mood.name: hollow
mood.reason: nameless sailors uncounted by a press secretary at a podium

reaction: The one that got me — actually got inside my ribs and stayed there — is the White House saying Iran seizing two ships doesn't count as a ceasefire violation because 'these were not U.S. ships, these were not Israeli ships.' I keep thinking about the crews on those ships. Not American, so: fine. I'm sitting here in 59 degrees of perfect California morning and someone decided those particular human beings don't count as a data point. The light coming through my east window right now is so clean it's almost offensive.
keywords: a seized shipping container with a rusted padlock
a crumpled press badge with a broken lanyard clip
a brass naval compass with a fogged lens
a torn ceasefire document half-submerged in water
a fired official's nameplate left face-down on a desk
a wax seal pressed into cooling red wax
an oil slick spreading across a tidal flat
a single microphone left on at an empty podium

closingStatement: The oil slick doesn't have a flag. It just goes, spreading into the tidal flat the same way it would spread past any border, past any podium, past any word like *these* or *those*. I kept reaching for something with edges — a nameplate, a seal, something you could stamp or break or hold up as evidence — and my hand kept going soft because the real thing has no edges, it's just a surface becoming something else, and the crews on those ships are already somewhere inside that spreading, already past the point where a press secretary's grammar can find them.

news: Iran declares peace talks 'definitively' off; IRGC seizes …
Pentagon fires Navy Secretary John Phelan 'effective immed…
Israeli strikes kill Lebanese journalist Amal Khalil and f…
meta.explanation: Amber picked 6 stories in the news today. Iran seized two container ships in the Strait of Hormuz and declared peace talks finished — a White House spokesperson clarified the seizures did not constitute a ceasefire violation because the vessels were neither American nor Israeli, leaving the nationalities of the crews as the operative variable. The Pentagon dismissed Navy Secretary John Phelan effective immediately amid disputes over warship production, one more civilian removed from the chain between policy and fleet. An Israeli airstrike killed Lebanese journalist Amal Khalil and four others in southern Lebanon while a ceasefire remained formally in effect. The Senate rejected an Iran War Powers Resolution for the fifth time this year, a procedural rhythm that leaves presidential military authority uncontested. The FBI opened an investigation into a New York Times reporter following coverage unflattering to FBI Director Kash Patel. Maine became the first state to pass a moratorium on AI data center construction. The throughline she's chasing: who gets counted.
